channel.title: Nature Communications
channel.description:
Nature Communications is an open access, multidisciplinary journal dedicated to publishing high-quality research in all areas of the biological, health, physical, chemical and Earth sciences. Papers published by the journal aim to represent important advances of significance to specialists within each field.

We are committed to providing an efficient service for both authors and readers. Our team of independent editors make rapid and fair publication decisions. Prompt dissemination of accepted papers to a wide readership and beyond is achieved through a programme of continuous online publication.
channel.dc:rights: © 2026 Macmillan Publishers Limited, part of Springer Nature. All rights reserved.
channel.prism:copyright: © 2026 Macmillan Publishers Limited, part of Springer Nature. All rights reserved.

item.title: [6] Sub-picosecond extraction of hot carriers in black phosphorus
item.content:encoded:
Nature Communications, Published online: 13 May 2026; doi:10.1038/s41467-026-72892-w

Here, the authors observe the excitation of ultrafast photocurrents in black phosphorus flakes with decay times of ~ 400 fs (3-dB bandwidth of 260 GHz), showing the potential of this 2D material for sub-picosecond hot-carrier extraction and high-speed optoelectronic devices.
item.dc:title: [6] Sub-picosecond extraction of hot carriers in black phosphorus
item.dc:creator: Katsumasa Yoshioka, Taro Wakamura, Takuya Okamoto, Norio Kumada
Taro Wakamura
Takuya Okamoto
Norio Kumada
item.description:
Last authors: Takuya Okamoto, Norio Kumada

Authors: Katsumasa Yoshioka, Taro Wakamura, Takuya Okamoto, Norio Kumada

Score 6/10 C_MAYBE

Why: Ultrafast carrier extraction in black phosphorus; relevant to the user's broad interest in 2D materials.

Tags: #2Dmaterials #hotcarriers #blackphosphorus

Abstract: Nature Communications, Published online: 13 May 2026; doi:10.1038/s41467-026-72892-w Here, the authors observe the excitation of ultrafast photocurrents in black phosphorus flakes with decay times of ~ 400 fs (3-dB bandwidth of 260 GHz), showing the potential of this 2D material for sub-picosecond hot-carrier extraction and high-speed optoelectronic devices.
